data.title: Why HealthTech? Why HEAL?
data.description:
The healthcare industry faces a pivotal moment as traditional care models struggle to meet growing demand. While preventive approaches have improved chronic condition management and patient outcomes, the gap between healthcare supply and demand continues to expand. Technology is becoming the essential connection across this divide, with the healthtech market expected to grow dramatically from about $225 billion in 2022 to over $1 trillion by 2032.1

This expansion is expected to encompass four key areas: healthcare analytics and software solutions, technology-enabled consumer healthcare, smart medical devices, and AI-enabled drug discovery. Each area represents a unique approach to expanding provider capacity and improving care delivery efficiency. While healthcare has historically been cautious in adopting new technologies, mounting pressure to serve an aging population, manage chronic conditions,2 and improve access to care is accelerating digital transformation across the industry.

In response to these trends, we’ve updated the Global X Telemedicine & Digital Health ETF (EDOC) to the Global X HealthTech ETF (HEAL), replacing the index the fund seeks to track effective April 1, 2025. This change reflects the rapidly expanding healthtech landscape and broadens our investment scope in an effort to capture more opportunities across the entire digital health ecosystem. HEAL seeks to provide investors with comprehensive exposure to companies driving healthcare’s technological revolution, positioning them to potentially benefit from the industry’s projected growth.

AI-Enabled Drug Discovery

Despite technological advancements, developing a new medicine still takes 10–15 years and costs $1.3 billion on average.4,5 Only one in ten investigational drugs, however, makes it to market.6 By running millions of scenarios, AI software could reduce the cost of preclinical drug development by 20-40% as well as accelerate design and validation of drug candidates up to 15-fold.7,8

The drug development process is complex, requiring careful validation at each stage to ensure that only the most promising candidates advance to clinical trials and U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) review. A flawed assumption or connection made in the early stages can result in years of wasted development time and resources on a drug that ultimately proves ineffective for patients. AI technology offers a potential solution by enabling researchers to leverage vast amounts of health data to make more informed decisions from the very beginning of the drug development process. This data-driven approach helps identify potential issues earlier and increases the likelihood of selecting successful drug candidates.

AI drug discovery software companies like Schrodinger, Simulation Plus, and Absci are pioneering a rapidly expanding industry. These providers can rapidly evaluate billions of potential compounds and predict how molecules will behave in the body. Further, they can harness machine learning to analyze massive biomedical datasets, revealing previously hidden patterns in disease mechanisms and optimizing drug designs digitally before any physical testing occurs.

The AI-enabled drug discovery software sector is projected to be the fastest-growing generative AI segment through 2032, with a compound annual growth rate of 121%.9 HEAL offers exposure to such software providers.

This industry is, in turn, also expected to benefit:

  • Pharmaceutical Companies: The integration of AI could fundamentally improve the economics of drug development. Currently, pharmaceutical companies must invest in hundreds of drug candidates knowing that only a small fraction will reach the market. This means approved drug prices must account for both successful and failed development costs. With AI-enhanced success rates, companies could develop more drugs more efficiently, potentially leading to better returns on investment and more affordable medicines.
  • Technology Infrastructure Providers: The tech industry is making significant investments in healthcare AI capabilities. Chip manufacturers, in particular, are prioritizing healthcare development to meet growing drug discovery demands. For example, Nvidia has established partnerships with hundreds of pharmaceutical and genomic firms, with their healthcare division now generating an estimated $1 billion in annual revenue.10

Smart Medical Devices

Smart medical devices are being deployed across the healthcare system to help scale up supply. These often apply novel software and AI applications to innovative hardware to help fill gaps in care. The FDA has approved approximately 950 AI-enabled medical devices in the United States, a dramatic increase from just 6 approved devices a decade ago.3, 11

Smart medical wearables can bring healthcare technology directly to patients for continuous monitoring without sacrificing comfort. Diabetes devices from companies like Tandem Diabetes and Dexcom have paved the way in this category. Their success — evidenced by a 67% reduction in hospitalizations — creates a foundation for expanded monitoring across cardiovascular and neurological conditions.12 Multi-biomarker tracking devices now chart the course for wearable health technology across diverse therapeutic applications.

Surgical robots have also been increasingly expanding their use cases. Though surgical robots have been in use for over two decades, advances in technology and software integration now enable more complex procedures. Benefits include reduced hospital stays, minimal scarring, lower infection risks, and decreased post-operative pain.13 Accessibility has improved through alternative financing options, with leasing arrangements accounting for approximately 60% of placements by industry leader Intuitive Surgical.14 Since receiving the first surgical robot approval in 2000, Intuitive Surgical has expanded to nearly 10,000 installations and facilitates over 2.6 million annual procedures.15

Similar devices are also being deployed behind the scenes, perhaps where patients might not directly see them in action. Healthcare facilities are rapidly adopting behind-the-scenes automation technologies. In the expanding $90 billion pharmacy automation market, integrated dispensing systems are revolutionizing medication management. These systems – like Omnicell’s – can address critical demand as 85% of U.S. hospitals face pharmacy technician shortages.16 Modern solutions, such as Omnicell’s automated dispensing systems, deliver significant improvements – increasing inventory capacity by 30% and reducing pharmacist workload by 75%.17,18

Healthcare Analytics & Software Solutions

While medical technology has advanced rapidly in areas like genomics and surgical robots, fundamental administrative processes remain surprisingly analog. An estimated 80% of U.S. healthcare documentation still travels via fax and traditional mail.19 This disconnect between advanced medical capabilities and outdated administrative processes creates significant challenges for healthcare providers and patients alike:

  • Physicians spend nearly 40% of their time on documentation rather than patient care.20
  • Three-quarters of medical staff report that documentation requirements actively interfere with patient care.21
  • More than three-quarters of physicians complete their documentation outside of regular hours.22
  • Nearly 80% of doctors experience burnout related to healthcare IT systems.23

AI-powered solutions are now bridging this divide through intelligent workflow automation. Advanced natural language processing – like the software capabilities offered by Doximity – can help generate clinical documentation, from progress notes to discharge summaries. Enterprise content management platforms – like Veeva’s flagship Vault – can help manage regulatory documentation and clinical trial data. Patient recruitment and retention software for clinical trials – such as IQVIA’s CORE technology solutions – seek to support clinical trial enrollment, a critical need given that an estimated 80% of trials miss their enrollment deadlines.24 AI chatbots – like Tempus’ innovative Tempus One platform – can instantly summarize relevant clinical research and genomic insights to help physicians make more informed treatment decisions for their patients.

These innovations extend beyond mere efficiency – they’re reshaping how the healthcare industry interacts with information.

The impact reaches beyond individual practices or businesses. By aggregating de-identified clinical data across diverse populations, these platforms create a living knowledge network. Providers can access insights from millions of patient encounters, stay current with emerging research, and make evidence-based decisions aligned with the latest standards of care. This collective intelligence, delivered through privacy-preserving analytics, is establishing a new foundation for medical decision-making.

Healthcare Analytics & Software Solutions

The rise of digital health solutions is fundamentally reshaping how patients access and experience healthcare. The global telemedicine market is projected to reach $280 billion by 2032, reflecting growing demand for convenient, accessible care options.25 While virtual care adoption accelerated during the COVID-19 pandemic, the sector has evolved far beyond basic video consultations.

Digital pharmacies are revolutionizing medication management, with platforms like Hims & Hers now serving over 2 million subscribers.26 These services combine home delivery with AI-powered medication management systems, reducing errors and improving adherence. Meanwhile, online healthcare marketplaces are streamlining how patients find and book care, significantly reducing wait times for specialist appointments.

Emerging hybrid care models blend virtual and in-person services, with a majority of healthcare organizations now offering some form of hybrid care delivery. These solutions particularly benefit chronic condition management, where continuous monitoring and regular provider check-ins can significantly improve outcomes. Remote patient monitoring programs have shown 50% reduction in hospital readmissions for conditions like heart failure and diabetes.27

Conclusion

The healthcare industry is experiencing unprecedented transformation through innovative technologies like artificial intelligence, robotics, and smart devices. These technological advancements are creating entirely new solutions across healthcare analytics, consumer healthcare, medical devices, and drug discovery — all while maintaining robust data protection standards essential for patient privacy and security.

HEAL offers investors a vehicle to target this opportunity by providing exposure to companies that operate at the intersection of healthcare and technology sectors. As the healthtech landscape evolves, HEAL offers access to innovations that may deliver growth potential while addressing important healthcare challenges.

data.title: Realty Income: T-Bill Returns With More Risk Is A Mediocre Mix
data.description:
Oh, is this one going to ruffle some feathers! Or should I say "O" since I'm talking about Realty Income (NYSE:O), the self-proclaimed "Monthly Dividend Company." The company has a well-earned reputation as a solid operator and industry leader. That monthly dividend has made it a fan favorite on this platform. Which is why my take on it might be met with more jeers than cheers. But hey, someone has to be the recipient of all of that vitriol, so it might as well be me.

Why would I doubt the allure of a monthly dividend REIT, one of the top 10 US publicly traded real estate companies, with a straightforward "triple net lease" structure? After all, the company has so much of what I like. Profitability, outstanding growth within its sector and a valuation grade that is above average. That, at a time when it seems every blue-chip stock I follow has a D or F valuation grade.

I'll finish our take on the business, then bottom-line the reason while I'd not dissuade any investor who studied O and buys it or owns it already from keeping it in their portfolio, I simply think I can do better. Or at least, the same, using a REIT ETF.

Again, there's nothing glaring about the business here that I dislike. But as I have and will continue to do here, I will try to raise investor awareness about how few stocks as a percentage of the US market actually add alpha versus and ETF. At least, at this stage of the market cycle. I came to this conclusion the hard way, by finding over and over in my own investing that too often, the gains I had on stocks were in sync or inferior to what would have happened if I had...pardon the expression...ETF'd it instead.

My goodness, is that a great tenant roster!

Most of its tenants are very well known by investors and are also very consolidated in their markets, such as retailers like Dollar General (DG), 7-Eleven (OTCPK:SVNDY), or other companies focused on experience and other face-to-face needs like Lifetime Fitness and FedEx (FDX).

Sure, there's macroeconomic sensitivity in that group. However, the tenant base is generally made up of strong businesses, focused on services or relatively essential services. Although very mature, Realty Income continues to invest in new verticals, such as gaming, data centers and international expansion, supporting a better outlook for continued growth in the coming decades through this TAM expansion and efficient capital allocation.

Realty Income's balance sheet is also quite strong, with long and well-distributed debt over the next decade and a leverage (net debt/adj. EBITDARE) of 5.4x

But what shines through is the company's track record, as shown in the image below. Realty Income has managed to consistently advance the growth of its AFFO/share, which is basically a proxy for shareholder value, i.e. how much cash its operations are generating, and this will consequently impact the dividends distributed. The CAGR is around 5%, as can be seen in the chart below.

Solid Margin Of Safety, But Could Be Better

The chart below shows how Realty Income has managed to generate shareholder value. One part is through a high dividend yield, and another is through AFFO/share growth.

I estimate that the optimal point to buy Realty Income is around a 6% forward dividend yield. Currently, the price-to-FFO of 13x and the forward dividend yield of 5.5% are already quite attractive for a market short of good alternative stocks with a high dividend yield and high quality.

These figures are enough to sustain an exposure to the REIT, but they are not that great. The $3.22 dividend forward estimate per stock takes us to this dividend yield of 5.5%. When the REIT was at $53, it was possible to capture a 6.08% dividend yield with the same assumptions, a level closer to the historical one and which allowed for an even better margin of safety.

The yield is close to its 7-year-high, so this is a stock that I would start to consider as a buy in my Yield At a Reasonable Price (YARP) methodology. But the stock is somewhat range bound. So if I'm OK with a 5-6% yield and potential price volatility, it's a "go." But I'm not, so it isn't a go.

Can't I change my mind on this popular yield stock?

Here's where the rubber meets the road for me. As I've noted in the past, I strongly believe the markets' functionality shifted dramatically starting in the first full year after the pandemic began. So, the start of 2021. Since that time, about 4 years, ago, O has seen its 3-year annualized return drop from a Covid-recovery high of 16% to below zero. Does that make a "screaming buy?" Not to me. It makes it an "if I have to own a REIT and not a REIT ETF" buy. But as shown below, this is not just a one timeframe issue.

Those rolling periods of 3 years each have seen O underperform both the cap weighted and equal weighted REIT sector ETFs far more often than not. And nearly continuously for a few years. Of course, this can change. But remember, this has been a period of generally good operating results for O. This is not a company-specific problem, it is the modern markets at work.

Because for every self-directed investor that enjoys the feeling of getting that monthly paycheck from being an O shareholder, there is a massive amount of index fund money (in S&P 500 ETFs that own REITs, and in REIT-focused ETFs) that does not care what it is buying. It just buys the index! That's a big reason in my view why O and many other REITs don't outperform as easily as they used to.

Here is the same type of chart, but using a different REIT index, since the pair I used above do not go back far enough in time. This is a very different look to me. I can see why so many income investors became enamored with O.

But now, monthly income is available in many forms, via ETFs. Without stock specific risk. Since that post-pandemic period began at the start of 2021, O has been neck and neck with T-bills. I think it will likely outperform T-bills the next 5 years, but not by such a wide margin that I'd assume the risk that comes with it.

This is actually a constructive chart. I could easily see O making a move up toward the $65 level, even to $75 over the next few years. That, plus that monthly dividend, is a solid double-digit percent return. But that doesn't make O a buy, just a hold. Because if that move were to occur, my suspicion is I could do better. My current REIT sector holding is Crown Castle (CCI), and I like American Tower (AMT) as well, but don't currently own it.

But those are different types of REITs, and my CCI position is collared with a put option purchased, and a call option sold against it. So that's a very different reward/risk tradeoff I've set up for myself.

Good company, decent stock, not a buy for me

So I land on a hold rating for O. That bores me as much as it probably bores the legion of fans that rave about this stock. I "get" the raving, just not the analysis that concludes that this stock is poised to become an alpha generator. That's what I'm looking for when I buy individual stocks in any sector.

data.title: How Companies Are Planning For 'Liberation Day'
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Tariff time

It's finally here. President Trump's "Make America Wealthy Again Event" will take place in the Rose Garden at 4:00 PM ET on Wednesday, which will be closely watched by investors and the business community. Uncertainty has plagued markets over the past month and a half, so even if things are drastic, the clarity might provide some relief. The event will allow companies to begin planning for the new tariff regime and factor the duties into their supply chains or models.

'Liberation Day': There has been a whole slew of reports of what will and won't be included in today's announcement. So far, the Trump administration has revealed that tariffs will "start with all countries" and will be "effective immediately." There might also be a range of rates that are dependent on trade barriers or taxation levels, with the caps applied to either specific or groups of countries. Besides the new tariffs, "25% levies on cars not made in the U.S." and "secondary tariffs on nations that purchase crude oil from Venezuela" are set to go into effect this week, and there might be more details on those fronts as well.

While around half of the 3,000 respondents to the latest Wall Street Breakfast survey think the current setup of international trade is unfair, only a quarter feel that tariffs represent the right strategy to put it back on track. On the corporate side, companies have been planning to respond to the tariffs in five different ways, or a combination of the following approaches.

1) Reshoring or boosting U.S. production activity ==> Johnson & Johnson's recent $55B investment

2) Pushing suppliers to reduce prices ==> Walmart with Chinese manufacturers

3) Raising prices to preserve profit margins ==> Target's warning for shoppers

4) Absorbing the tariffs with lower margins ==> Latest guidance from Nike and FedEx

5) Lobbying for certain exemptions to limit their impact ==> Pursued by U.S. automakers

Market reaction? Watch futures and after-hours trading activity as Trump's announcement will take place after the market close, though the White House has referred to the recent selloff as only a "snapshot in time." "There has been a lot of talk about the market, and it was up yesterday," Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt declared at her latest briefing. "The President wants to make sure all Americans make out well, particularly Main Street. That's the focus of these tariffs, but like I've said repeatedly, just like they were in his first term, Wall Street will be just fine."

What else is happening...

Tepid IPO? CoreWeave (CRWV) soars on third day of trading.

Princeton considers $320M bond sale amid funding freeze.

Visa, Amex vie to swipe Apple credit card from Mastercard.

TikTok's (BDNCE) fate hangs in the balance as deadline nears.

Tesla (TSLA) deliveries on watch amid 'Tesla Takedown' protests.

ChatGPT usage said to hit record after rollout of Ghibli effect.

Moody’s goes big! Recession odds now at 40% for 2025.

Report: Republicans are eyeing a $25,000 SALT cap.

USDC-USD stablecoin issuer Circle files for long-awaited IPO.

Germany deploys first permanent brigade abroad since WW2.

In Asia, Japan +0.3%. Hong Kong flat. China +0.1%. India +0.8%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.8%. Paris -0.6%. Frankfurt -1.3%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -0.4%. S&P -0.5%. Nasdaq -0.6%. Crude -0.4% to $70.92. Gold +0.6% to $3,163.20. Bitcoin +0.9% to $84,987.
Ten-year Treasury Yield -2 bps to 4.16%.

data.title: Important Warning: 3 Popular Dividend Stocks Due For A Sharp Pullback
data.description:
Blue-chip dividend stocks that have a strong track record of consistently paying out and even growing their dividends while also having a durable, defensive underlying business model and a sound balance sheet are very popular with retail dividend investors. This is epitomized by the strong popularity of gold standard dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD):

There is a good reason for this, especially if you are looking to retire on passive income, as many dividend investors are. Having stocks that you can count on through thick and thin to deliver reliable income that grows at a pace that can keep up with inflation and help meet your living expenses over time is invaluable.

That being said, just because a stock is a dependable dividend payer and the underlying business model and balance sheet are in sound shape does not mean that it makes a good investment—or even that you should never sell it. Of course, everyone's tax circumstances are unique, and sometimes your capital gains are so high in a stock that it does not make sense to sell it. But if you hold it in a tax-advantaged account or your tax circumstances are such that the tax hit is not too great, I am a firm believer in trimming or even outright selling dividend stocks whenever they become meaningfully overvalued.

After all, one of the big advantages of investing in publicly traded securities is that it is very easy and efficient to get in and out of positions. Therefore, if you hold a stock that is meaningfully overvalued, and you see a peer company that is similar in quality but yet trades at a meaningful discount to it, and the tax consequences are not significant, it makes all the sense in the world to sell your existing position and recycle the capital into the other one.

With that view in mind, in this article, I am going to discuss three popular dividend stocks that have become overvalued and are due for a sharp pullback.

Overvalued Dividend Stock #1

The first popular dividend stock I am going to talk about today is Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL). It is a blue-chip business development company (BIZD) that is managed by the world's largest alternative asset manager, Blackstone (BX). The advantages it gets from this relationship are made clear by its impressive track record since going public of growing shareholder value and delivering strong total returns.

On top of that, its underwriting performance is very impressive, with just 0.3% of the portfolio at amortized cost on non-accrual. The portfolio is also very defensively positioned, with 98% of it invested in first-lien senior secured loans. It also grew its NAV per share at 3% on a year-over-year basis in its most recent quarter, and its base dividend is adequately covered with a 1.09x coverage ratio. The balance sheet is investment-grade, with leverage at 1.17x, which is right in the middle of its target range. Additionally, only $1.5 billion out of its $9.3 billion total committed debt matures over the next two years.

However, its valuation has gotten a bit rich, as it currently trades at a 19% premium to its book value as of this writing, which is quite high relative to many of its peers. For example, Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL) has a very similar portfolio makeup, fee structure, and underwriting performance as BXSL, yet it trades roughly in line with its net asset value—putting it at a near 20% discount to BXSL. Sure, BXSL may benefit from being externally managed by BX, which some may consider to be a superior external manager to MSDL's Morgan Stanley (MS), but MS is still a very high-quality external manager given its status as a global investment bank. Additionally, MSDL's track record as both a private and public BDC indicates that it is a high-quality fund. As a result, I do not think that a nearly 20% difference in valuation multiples is warranted here.

On top of that, BXSL faces several headwinds that, if evaluated objectively on a standalone basis rather than just on a relative basis to other BDCs, imply it could be meaningfully overvalued. For example, the chances of Fed rate cuts this year are growing as concerns about the health of the economy mount due to Trump’s tariff policies and weakening consumer confidence. Additionally, BXSL has several debt maturities this year, including some of its low-cost fixed-rate debt. While not a huge percentage of its overall debt profile, it still provides a headwind, as BXSL will very likely have to refinance that debt at materially higher interest rates. When you combine the potential for rate cuts with rising interest expense from refinancing its long-term fixed-rate debt—while virtually all of its loan portfolio is floating rate and indexed to short-term rates—profit spreads may decline. BXSL could also see an increase in non-accruals if the economy weakens further. As a result, BXSL is likely due for a material pullback in its stock price as its NAV may weaken and its price-to-NAV multiple will likely contract to be more in line with peers as well as in response to sentiment for the sector potentially deteriorating if the economy stumbles.

Overvalued Dividend Stock #2

The next stock I am going to discuss is The Williams Companies (WMB). It is a midstream infrastructure company (MLPX) focused on natural gas transportation. It has a strong track record of growing its dividend over time and owns some crown-jewel natural gas infrastructure assets that are expected to profit from the AI data center boom, while also giving it a long runway of growth investment opportunities with attractive returns. In fact, the company is seeing the ability to generate over 20% cash returns on investment right now, which means it is able to generate strong growth from cash that it retains and reinvests in its business.

While WMB has a strong track record, a defensive and durable business model, a solid balance sheet with a BBB credit rating, a 3.55x expected leverage ratio this year, and an 11.3-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt—with 79% of its transmission revenue coming from investment-grade counterparties—I still think WMB is positioned for a substantial pullback. Here is why.

First, midstream infrastructure is a capital-intensive industry, and despite its strong returns on investment and long growth runway, WMB is unlikely to generate any sort of outstanding growth acceleration. This is reflected in management guidance for mid-single-digit CAGR for the foreseeable future, as well as analyst consensus estimates that put distributable cash flow per share growth at just a 5.2% CAGR through 2029.

That is certainly inflation-beating, but when you combine that with the valuation—which currently features a dividend yield at a paltry 3.4% and an EV/EBITDA multiple of 13.28x, compared to its historic average dividend yield of 5.64% and average EV/EBITDA of just 11.81x—WMB looks overvalued right now.

On top of that, you can compare WMB to other midstream natural gas players like Kinder Morgan (KMI), which has a similar growth profile and a similarly strong natural gas portfolio, yet trades at a much more attractive valuation, with a 4.1% dividend yield and 11.59x EV/EBITDA. As a result, I think WMB is due for a material pullback in terms of its valuation, which should bring its current cash flow yield and dividend yield more in line with peers and its more modest growth profile.

Overvalued Dividend Stock #3

The third stock I am going to talk about today is AT&T (T), which currently appears excessively overvalued given its weak growth outlook. For example, it currently offers a 3.93% dividend yield, while its historic average is 5.95%. Its EV/EBITDA multiple at the moment is 7.87x, which is well above its historic average of 6.79x.

With that kind of valuation premium, you would think that growth would be accelerating sharply for them. However, analysts expect the dividend to grow at just a 0.8% CAGR through 2029, and earnings per share will likely be pretty flat through 2026 before accelerating somewhat to bring in a 5.1% CAGR through 2029.

On top of that, AT&T looks even more overvalued when comparing it to Verizon (VZ), which currently offers a 6% dividend yield and trades at a 7.2x EV/EBITDA multiple. Historically, VZ has had a higher EV/EBITDA than AT&T, with a 6.95x average compared to T's 6.79x, and has also had a lower dividend yield historically at 5.05% compared to T’s 5.95%.

This makes AT&T look even more overvalued at the moment. Furthermore, VZ is known for having the best wireless network in North America, which should give it a competitive advantage over T, and its dividend and earnings per share are expected to grow at a fairly similar clip to T's in the coming years.

As a result, I think that T deserves a sharp pullback to bring its valuation more in line with a peer like VZ, as well as to reflect a more realistic overall total return profile given where interest rates are right now.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, while BXSL, WMB, and T have delivered solid results recently and combine durable and growing dividends with strong defensive business models and healthy balance sheets, all three are priced well above peers as well as what seems reasonable for their businesses relative to current interest rates and their respective growth potentials. As a result, I think that all three are due for sharp corrections, and investors should consider this a warning against falling in love with slow-growth dividend stocks that have outperformed in the recent past to the point of becoming overvalued today. These are the sorts of stocks that we rotate out of and recycle the capital into undervalued yet similarly high-quality peer opportunities in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: The Great Transformation: Riding The Wave Of Change
data.description:
Welcome to 2025, where change isn’t just constant — it’s accelerating. For investors, success means surfing these transformative waves, not merely weathering them.

Climate: From Concern to Crisis

2024 broke all temperature records (again), making it the hottest year in 125,000 years. Yet, humanity responded by… burning more fossil fuels. It’s like drinking more coffee to cure insomnia. And yet, the picture is not monolithic, 2025 and beyond will remain dynamic to say the least.

What’s coming:

  • $1.8T in climate adaptation spending by 2027.
  • Policy divergence between regions. While Europe and parts of Asia step on the green accelerator, others are pumping the brakes. Invest accordingly.
  • Insurance industry is fundamentally reassessing risk. After record disaster payouts, they’re fundamentally rethinking how they assess risk. This will ripple through real estate, municipal bonds, corporate planning, and more.

China’s Green Domination: The Dragon Goes Electric

Energy isn’t just a commodity anymore — it’s technology. And technology gets cheaper and better over time. China has spotted this trend and is running with it like an Olympic sprinter.

The scorecard:

  • China’s renewable energy dominance: 80% of global solar panels, 70% of battery production, and a rapidly growing EV industry. It’s less of a race and more of a “everyone else is hoping for silver.”
  • Solar expansion unstoppable: Global capacity could double by 2027, with China at the center, despite trade tensions.
  • Battery breakthroughs incoming: Costs dropping below $70/kWh by 2027, with innovations beyond lithium-ion hitting the market.
  • Grid modernization is desperately needed: A multi-trillion-dollar opportunity as our electrical systems struggle to keep up with renewables.

Global Trade: Tensions Boiling

Trump’s return has intensified trade conflicts. China’s stimulus response makes the New Deal investments and reforms to exit the Great Depression look modest.

Watch for:

  • Economic restructuring beyond short-term tariff reactions
  • Market volatility amid continued technological progress
  • Selective decoupling in critical sectors
  • Capital flows shifting toward Asian innovation centers

Fiscal Reality: Creative Debt Management

U.S. Federal government debt has reached the level where interest payments are now the largest single line item, yet plans to address this are unclear.

The new normal:

  • Germany’s €1.2T spending shift signals European policy transformation
  • Innovation in debt management, rather than reduction
  • Healthcare demands are intensifying with aging populations
  • Potential productivity boost from AI, renewables, and automation

Tech Revolution: AI Enters Physical World

AI is moving beyond chatbots to transform physical systems and supercharge human capabilities.

Coming attractions:

  • Innovation is flowing across borders despite politics
  • AI integration into robotics and infrastructure
  • Economic transformation through convergence of renewables, automation, and AI
  • Job market evolution is creating new categories of work

Biotech: Biology Becomes Technology

Biotechnology’s “iPhone moment” is creating cross-sector disruption.

The biological revolution:

  • Personalized medicine is becoming standard
  • Engineered crops and precision farming are boosting productivity
  • Bio-based manufacturing processes are disrupting traditional industries
  • Novel biological materials are creating new markets

Investment Playbook

Smart investors will focus on signals, not noise.

Action plan:

  • Look beyond borders to diverse innovation centers
  • Focus on fundamental technological transformations
  • Bet on climate adaptation infrastructure
  • Follow bioeconomy leaders
  • Embrace supply chain innovation

Conclusion

We’re at an economic inflection point. Headlines trumpet geopolitical drama, but the real story is deeper transformation. China’s technology leadership and Europe’s fiscal awakening suggest a multipolar future. Climate challenges are driving unprecedented innovation, while technological convergence creates immense opportunities.

Success depends on identifying emerging leaders regardless of nationality and focusing on long-term value creation.

The waves of change are big, but rideable — with the right investment framework.

Disclosure: None

Original Post

data.title: TCW Concentrated Large Cap Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

The Fund (I Share) generated a net return of +5.78% during the fourth quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index return of +7.07%. Relative underperformance was primarily due to negative sector allocation effects (primarily in the consumer discretionary, healthcare, and real estate sectors). For the full year, the Fund (I Share) generated a return of +30.92%, underperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index return of +33.36%. Negative sector allocation effects outweighed positive security selection effects in CY2024.

Investment Environment

Equity markets moved higher in the fourth quarter. The NASDAQ advanced 6.3%, the S&P 500 +2.4%, and the DJIA +0.5%. The price of crude increased 6.9% and was up 10.1% in 2024. For the quarter, gold dropped 1.6% but was up 20.3% for the year. Economic growth has largely supported the stock market advance, as the U.S. consumer has remained somewhat resilient in the face of sticky inflation and diminished purchasing power. Essential items cost increases (food, clothing, energy, shelter, insurance, utilities) have exceeded headline CPI (Consumer Price Index) in 42 of the past 47 months. Wage increases have failed to keep up with inflation.

Corporate profits remain strong, however, and U.S. investment grade corporate spreads are near multi-decade tights. December’s ISM survey was in contraction territory (49.3), and the most recent U.S. housing starts data (November) indicated housing starts are at the lowest level since July 2024. After a 50 bps rate cut in September, the FOMC cut another 25 bps in each of November and December, and the Federal Reserve (Fed) noted it may only cut rates two more times in 2025. Inflation forecasts moved up for 2025 with the Fed’s preferred inflation measurement, headline PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures), estimated to increase 2.5% vs 2.1% prior. Fed Chairman Powell’s hawkish commentary in December that the Fed’s year-end inflation forecast has “kind of fallen apart” was noteworthy. As we enter 2025, we are closely monitoring yields on the 10-year UST (up over 100 bps since the Fed’s September rate cut), as well as whether Trump 2.0 leads to deregulation, lower taxes, and an acceleration in economic growth or whether tariffs create unintended consequences.

Sector Weightings

Including the contribution of sector allocation and security selection, our consumer discretionary and financials weightings hurt relative results most, and our information technology and consumer staples weightings most positively impacted relative performance during the fourth quarter.

Security Selection*

Our strongest performance during the period came from the information technology sector. After underperforming the broader market through the first half of 2024, shares of ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW; 7.41%**) moved higher in the fourth quarter after the company reported strong quarterly results in October that topped consensus estimates and the company raised forward guidance. Most of the large deals signed during the quarter were multi-platform (e.g. 18 of top 20 deals included seven products), the Federal business was solid (no impact from Carahsoft, according to management), and the company’s AI SKU (Now Assist) remains the fastest growing product in NOW’s history. With all the focus on hyperscalers in the early innings of this AI boom, there has been consternation amongst some investors around enterprise software and how soon AI investments will be monetized, but NOW is delivering (NOW was able to sign 44 deals over $1 mm, six deals over $5 mm and two over $10 mm). Customers who use Now Assist are receiving tangible returns on their investments. Given the high customer ROI, we believe this positions NOW to capture burgeoning AI spending. The company’s Xanadu release (released in early September) included 350 new GenAI use cases, allowing AI agents to work seamlessly alongside human employees and increase employee productivity. We remain bullish on NOW shares, given our belief the current share price underestimates the long-term earnings power of the company. Shares of Shopify Inc. (SHOP; 2.52%**) rallied after the company reported strong quarterly results that topped consensus estimates. Against a macro backdrop of slowing e-commerce growth, SHOP’s revenue increased 26% YoY, volume growth accelerated, and international growth was robust. Shop Pay volume increased 42% YoY and is approaching half of SHOP’s GPV (Gross Payment Volume). The company continues to add enterprise merchants to the platform, and MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue) increased 24% YoY. When we first purchased shares earlier this year, we were attracted to the SHOP’s accelerating market share capture, capital-light business model, pricing power and leverage to the secular trend of Direct-to-Consumer retail. We remain positive on shares.

Our weakest relative performance during the fourth quarter came from the information technology sector. Shares of Broadcom Inc. (AVGO; 2.11%**) rallied during the period after the company reported quarterly earnings. (Though we recently initiated our position in the security, our underweight relative to the Russell 1000® Growth Index hurt our relative performance during the quarter.) While results for AVGO’s fiscal fourth quarter largely met consensus estimates, management’s guidance of a potential $60b-$90b in AI revenue in fiscal 2027 from existing customers led to the stock rally. For reference, AVGO’s AI revenue in fiscal 2024 was $12b, so this represents a meaningful increase in AI revenue over the next three years. As large cloud customers continue to build out larger and larger clusters, AVGO stands to benefit from the increased complexity through both custom ASICs and a variety of other leading networking solutions. While we recognize revenue could be lumpy over the next several years, we are attracted to the company’s steady free cash flow generation and disciplined capital allocation, enabled by its market position in a secular growth end market. Shares of ASML Holding NV (ASML; 2.05%**) dropped after the company reported disappointing quarterly results that missed consensus expectations and management lowered forward guidance because of order pushouts at foundry/logic customers. As we wrote in a previous commentary, given Intel’s capex cut announcement as well as the potential for further export restrictions on ASML’s ability to ship and service products in China, there was a chance the company might have to lower guidance, but the magnitude of the miss seemed to catch some investors offsides. ASML needs €3-4bn of orders in the fourth quarter to hit the midpoint of its revised 2025 guidance, which we believe is achievable. China revenues are normalizing lower (from ~45-50% to ~20% in CY2025), while DUV growth ex-China remains strong. Also, ahead of ASML’s Investor Day in November, the company reiterated 2030 revenue guidance of €44bn - €60bn. While a wide range, the reiteration provided some relief for shares. We believe AI demand remains robust and ASML’s product roadmap is critical to supporting the increased demand for leading-edge semiconductors expected over the coming decade. We remain positive on shares.

Representative Buys (Ticker; Sector)*

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO; Information Technology) – Broadcom Inc. is a global technology company that designs, develops, and supplies a broad range of semiconductor and infrastructure software solutions. The company is the leading provider of custom silicon for AI, an end market we believe is in the early innings of growth with a rapidly expanding SAM (Service Addressable Market). As large cloud customers continue to build out larger and larger clusters, AVGO stands to benefit from the increased complexity through both custom ASICs and a variety of other leading networking solutions. Though lower growth, AVGO’s infrastructure software business (VMWare, acquired in 2023) acts as a cash machine with 70% standalone operating margins. We are attracted to the company’s steady free cash flow generation and disciplined capital allocation, enabled by its dominant market position in a secular growth end market. We believe the current share price does not adequately reflect the longer-term cash flow generation potential of the business.

Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL; Information Technology) (1.31%**) – Tyler Technologies provides mission-critical, vertical software solutions for the public sector, primarily state and local governments. Governments have lagged commercial industry in tech modernization and Tyler is a key beneficiary of the accelerating migrations of local and state governments to more modern, SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) and Cloud-based solutions that are finally happening. The company is also following the well understood business model migration path from license-based software to more recurring subscription revenue. Given the company’s focus on vertical market software for state and local governments and schools, the company enjoys a very sticky customer base with low churn and more limited economic sensitivity. Beyond its highly predictive business model, Tyler’s unique and broad platform create sufficient opportunities to continue to generate attractive growth for many years to come. We believe the durability and compounding potential of the company’s revenue, earnings and free cash flow are not fully appreciated.

Representative Sells (Ticker; Sector)*†

The Home Depot, Inc. (HD; Consumer Discretionary) – Home Depot is one of the largest retailers in the U.S. and the world’s largest home improvement specialty retailer. The company operates over 2,300 warehouse-format stores in North America and operates an online business. Home Depot targets the Do-It-Yourself (DIY) and professional services markets with a selection of over 40,000 items. When we first purchased shares over seven years ago, we were attracted to the company’s structural advantages over its leading competitors, including a higher professional services mix, higher ecommerce mix and demographically advantaged store footprint. Though we still believe HD possesses many positive attributes, the macro environment has pressured both growth and margins and our conviction level in the opportunity from here has waned. Accordingly, we have been using the position as a source of cash and recently sold the last of our position to fund ideas we believe provide more upside over the next three-to-five years.

Salesforce, Inc. (CRM; Information Technology) – Salesforce, Inc. provides cloud-based Customer Relationship Management (CRM) technology on top of an extensible and customizable platform. We have owned shares since 2005 and have been trimming shares as the growth algorithm has slowed over the last several years. Though we still believe CRM possesses many positive attributes, we ultimately decided to sell the last of our position to fund other more attractive ideas.

Snowflake Inc. (SNOW; Information Technology) – Snowflake Inc. is an enterprise software company focused on the data cloud. Its cloud-native architecture provides the capabilities of traditional data warehouses, data lakes and other data and analytics platforms while providing infrastructure independence through its multi-cloud support and a pricing model separating storage and compute. This separation allows for better optimization and scalability. SNOW’s architecture also allows for granularly provisioned, live data sharing via public clouds. When we first purchased shares, we were attracted to management’s ability to execute on a sizable and growing addressable market opportunity. Though we still believe SNOW possesses many positive attributes, challenging optimization trends and heightened competition (as AI reshapes the market) have pressured the growth profile of the company, and our conviction level in the opportunity from here has waned. Accordingly, we elected to sell our shares to fund other more attractive ideas.

Market Outlook

As we survey the landscape, there is so much about the current environment that is atypical. Historical analogues continue to prove inadequate. In early 2023, consensus suggested certain recession given an inverted yield curve, contractionary ISM measures, bank failures and a triggering of the so-called Sahm rule (i.e. a 50 bps increase in the unemployment rate). Despite the triggering of these traditionally useful measures, we have yet to register a recession 1. Consensus pivoted in 2024 to a soft or no-landing scenario as inflationary measures declined, and expectations grew for Fed rate cuts. These expectations too have been interrupted by inflation scares, higher long-term interest rates and questions about whether further short-term rate cuts are appropriate. Equity market indices have remained healthy despite these dynamics, but outside the largest index names, performance has signaled less sanguinity. As discussed in prior commentaries, market concentration levels are at the highest levels in decades (top 10 weightings constitute 39% of the S&P 500) and the decoupling of performance between the bigger weightings and others is striking (the equal-weighted S&P 500 underperformed the S&P 500 by ~12% in 2024).

Other anomalies have included the Fed’s 50 basis point rate cut in September, a striking move for a still growing economy with healthy employment trends. After the second longest Fed pause on record, a 4.1% U.S. unemployment rate, and PCE (the Fed’s preferred inflation measure) at 2.1%, the Fed elected to start cutting rates in September 2024. With the S&P 500 near an all-time high, this also marked the highest P/E at the start of a Fed cutting cycle in over 60 years. Also, we have never seen fiscal deficits as high as a percentage of GDP at the beginning of a rate cutting cycle. Current deficit levels have only been reached previously during recessions. We could easily spend the rest of this letter describing what makes the current environment new and different. Our point is simply that there is no clear playbook for the current environment, at least from a top-down perspective.

Yet, as we consider potential paths for both the economy and the market this year, there are many positives to consider. Corporate profits, for one, remain quite healthy. Although we might be a bit skeptical of the amount of acceleration in growth baked into S&P 500 earnings expectations this year, stable to improving outlooks across our portfolio of quality companies is encouraging. Additionally, the potential for several of the new administration’s priorities around reducing regulation and easing some of the heightened scrutiny on mergers and acquisitions should also be favorable tailwinds. While not all proposals will positively impact our portfolio companies (e.g. tariffs), we expect the headwinds to be manageable. Employment trends, balance sheet strength, and recent bookings trends across many of our companies also all indicate positive near-term health.

Balancing the positives, we are also considering what might derail a seemingly Goldilocks scenario priced into the broader market as we enter 2025. One scenario we are closely monitoring is the potential for a second wave of inflation. What if inflation is stubborn and the Fed is not able to cut further, and long-term interest rates continue to increase? Although not our base case, this is a scenario we have contemplated. As we learned in 2022, higher rates can quickly reverse the momentum in stock prices by affecting discount rates, even if fundamentals see limited impact. This new and higher real cost of capital forces the market to differentiate among business models to a much greater extent. If we had to choose between where rates stand today and rates during the ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy) era, we would choose today. Our investment philosophy drives us toward self-funding, asset-light, low-leverage, strong balance sheet, quality businesses with durable advantages that drive market share capture. The current higher cost of capital creates difficulties for lower quality companies in less attractive industries that may be more dependent upon the capital markets for funding. This creates a favorable environment for our high-quality businesses either because of the fundamental opportunities created or the scarcity value that can accrue to businesses with fewer challenges.

Ultimately, as we contemplate the coming year, our eyes are wide open to a range of possibilities. While we are optimistic the current positive trends remain for our companies, we are mindful that negative surprises can create hard pivots in consensus. Our north star remains identifying durably advantaged business models with large, attractive end markets. As long as we do not overpay for the business when we get the business model advantages and market opportunity right, we expect intrinsic value to compound each year at an attractive rate. Multiples may compress and expand from year-to-year skewing the realization of that intrinsic value in any given year, but over time, we believe performance in the portfolio should more closely match the multi-year earnings growth of our portfolio companies and generate attractive returns. While we cannot predict portfolio returns in any given year, we remain highly confident that our portfolio companies will continue to grow their intrinsic value in the coming quarters and years. This focus remains our primary playbook for the current environment.

As always, we remain truly grateful for your trust and support,

Brandon D. Bond, CFA Managing Director Portfolio Manager Equities

Brian M. McNamara Managing Director Portfolio Manager Equities

Bo Fifer, Managing Director Portfolio Manager Equities

* It should not be assumed that an investment in the securities listed was or will be profitable.

**Portfolio securities are stated as a percentage of the Fund’s total net assets including cash and cash equivalents as of December 31, 2024.

† Effective February 28, 2025, the name of the TCW Select Equities Fund changed to the TCW Concentrated Large Cap Growth Fund.

† Not held by the Fund as of December 31, 2024.

1 Historically, a recession has been defined as two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth. On July 21, 2022, the White House published a blog post titled, “How Do Economists Determine Whether the Economy Is in a Recession?”

Glossary of Terms

Artificial Intelligence (AI) – The theory and development of computer systems able to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making, and translation between languages. Balance Sheet– A financial statement that summarizes a company’s assets, liabilities and shareholders’ equity at a specific point in time. BPS (Basis Points)– A unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1%, and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security. Bullish– An upward trend in the prices of an industry’s stocks or the overall rise in broad market indices, characterized by high investor confidence. Cash Flow– The movement of money into or out of a business, project, or financial product. CPI (Consumer Price Index)– Measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services. Deficit– The amount by which expenses exceed income or costs outstrip revenues. Expense Ratio– A measure of what it costs an investment company to operate a mutual fund. Fed( Federal Reserve)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee)– The branch of the Federal Reserve Board that determines the direction of monetary policy. Free Cash Flow (FCF)– FCF represents the cash that a company is able to generate after spending the money required to maintain or expand its asset base. GDP (Gross Domestic Product)– The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period of time. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hawkishness– Generally favoring relatively high interest rates in order to keep inflation in check. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Nasdaq– Nasdaq is a global electronic marketplace for buying and selling securities, as well as the benchmark index for U.S. technology stocks. Nasdaq was created by the National Association of Securities Dealers (NASD) to enable investors to trade securities on a computerized, speedy and transparent system, and commenced operations on February 8, 1971. The term “Nasdaq” is also used to refer to the Nasdaq Composite, an index of more than 3,000 stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange that includes the world’s foremost technology and biotech giants such as Apple, Google, Microsoft, Oracle, Amazon, Intel and Amgen. PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditure)– The primary measure of consumer spending on goods and services in the U.S. economy. Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E)– A valuation ratio of a company’s current share price compared to its per-share earnings. Recession– Two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth as measured by a country’s gross domestic product. Return on Investment (ROI)- A performance measure used to evaluate the efficiency of an investment or to compare the efficiency of a number of different investments. Revenue– The value of all sales of goods and services recognized by a company in a period. Russell 1000® Growth Index– Measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher expected growth values. Small Capitalization (Small Cap)– Companies with a market capitalization of between $300 million and $2 billion. Soft Landing– A soft landing is the goal of a central bank when it seeks to raise interest rates just enough to stop an economy from overheating and experiencing high inflation, without causing a severe downturn. Standard & Poor’s 500 Index (S&P 500)– An index of 505 stocks issued by 500 large companies with market capitalizations of at least $6.1 billion. It is seen as a leading indicator of U.S. equities and a reflection of the performance of the large-cap universe. Underperform– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Unemployment Rate– The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment and willing to work. Value– A fund that primarily holds stocks that are deemed to be undervalued in price and that are likely to pay dividends. Volatility– The propensity of the value of an asset or market to rise or fall. Yield Curve– A line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates. The most frequently reported yield curve compares the three-month, two-year, five-year and 30-year U.S. Treasury debt. This yield curve is used as a benchmark for other debt in the market, such as mortgage rates or bank lending rates. The curve is also used to predict changes in economic output and growth. YoY– Year-over-year. YTD– Year-to-date. ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy)– A policy described by maintaining exceptionally low nominal interest rates used by central banks to stimulate economic growth during periods of prolonged economic slowdowns. The U.S. Federal Reserve Bank (the Fed) implemented ZIRP on December 16, 2008, in response to the global recession triggered by the financial crisis.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Big Retailers Ask China For Help Ahead Of Tariff Announcement
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Walmart (WMT) pushes Chinese suppliers to reduce prices amid Trump tariff fears - report. (00:23) LG Energy reaches deal to buy GM's stake in Michigan battery JV for $2B. (01:56) Visa (V), American Express (AXP) seek to swipe Apple (AAPL) credit card from Mastercard - report. (02:44)

This is an abridged transcript.

Walmart (NYSE:WMT) is reportedly actively pushing its Chinese suppliers to reduce prices in response to tariffs imposed by the Trump administration.

According to a report by Bloomberg that cites people familiar with the matter, the Chinese suppliers are struggling to comply with these demands due to already thin profit margins. Some Chinese manufacturers are having trouble meeting Walmart's (WMT) expectations to reduce costs by up to 10% for each round of tariffs.

The pressure on suppliers has led to varied responses, with some manufacturers concerned that any significant reduction in prices could lead them into financial losses.

A few Chinese factory owners have started to explore alternative manufacturing locations elsewhere in Asia, like Cambodia and Vietnam, to maintain both their long-term relationships with U.S. customers and profitability.

The sources added that Walmart (WMT) and its suppliers are negotiating across product categories, not only by country of origin.

Chinese state media criticized Walmart's (WMT) approach, suggesting that demanding such price cuts could disrupt fair competition and potentially harm both U.S. and Chinese businesses.

The retail giant has been diversifying its supply chain to reduce reliance on China, but the country remains a crucial source for various goods like clothing, toys, and electronics, among others.

It has been widely reported that Costco and Target are also pushing for discounts.

South Korea's LG Energy Solution said Tuesday it agreed to acquire the assets held by its Michigan electric vehicle battery joint venture with General Motors (NYSE:GM) for $2B.

GM (NYSE:GM) has been scaling back its EV plans because of uncertainty about the future of EV battery production and consumer tax credits under the Trump administration, saying in December that it planned to sell its stake in the Michigan plant.

LG said the transaction costs were part of its capital spending plan announced earlier this year, and could be lower than $2B.

The two companies continue to maintain two other U.S. battery plant joint ventures operating in Ohio and Tennessee.

With the acquisition, LG Energy is expected to produce batteries for Toyota (TM), including the supply of 20 GWh of nickel, cobalt, manganese, and aluminum battery modules annually, enough to power ~200K high-performance electric vehicles.

Visa (NYSE:V) has offered Apple (AAPL) ~$100M to switch its popular co-branded credit card to its payment network from Mastercard. (NYSE:MA), according to a media report.

The Wall Street Journal reported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter that American Express (NYSE:AXP) has been seeking the roles of both network provider and the card issuer.

There's already been reporting that JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Synchrony Financial (SYF) have been competing to replace Goldman Sachs (GS) as the bank issuing the Apple Card. The competition among banks is expected as Goldman (GS) pulls back from consumer finance.

Apple (AAPL), however, is expected to choose a network for its card before it selects the bank to replace Goldman (GS), the WSJ said.

Visa (NYSE:V) is aggressively pitching Apple (AAPL), making an offer similar to the upfront payment it offered when Costco selected a network for its card about 10 years ago. Mastercard (NYSE:MA), though, is fighting to keep its role as the card's network, the same people told the WSJ.

Big Pharma drops sharply ahead of Trump’s tariff announcement

Wells Fargo warns Tesla could fall 50% after deliveries, earnings, and the Cybercab rollout disappoint

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is flat at $71/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.8% at $85,000. Gold is up 0.5% at $3,100.

data.title: China Just Redrew The Aluminum Map, And The World Is Watching
data.description:
Original Post

By Sohrab Darabshaw

At a time when analysts expect the global demand for aluminum to rise, a new development in China may significantly impact both aluminum market demand and the country's internal consumption. That said, market watchers know that any announcement around metals coming out of Beijing these days also has to be looked at through the lens of the ongoing tariff war with the United States.

It's a pivotal moment for China's aluminum industry, even as the nation's production capacity nears its government-imposed annual limit of 45 million tons (MT). The government set that ceiling in order to address overcapacity and reduce emissions. However, it unveiled a fresh plan last week - one that spells out several "ambitious" new goals for the aluminum sector.

Anything that China says or does these days where metals and minerals are concerned makes experts and markets sit up and take note. China is the largest producer and one of the biggest consumers of aluminum, producing about 43 MT annually. As a result, experts and analysts are now scurrying to make sense of the MIIT report and its potential impact on aluminum and bauxite, the latter being the primary source of alumina.

China Intent on Remaining the Global Aluminum Market Leader

While it may be far too early to accurately predict the final outcome of this new plan, initial reactions by a handful of analysts have said that it could be a two-fold attempt by China. They theorize the plan will not only streamline the industry, but allow the country to go "all in" on a greener method of production, thus helping it retain its leadership position in the aluminum market.

Only time will tell how it will pan out, but the anticipated slowdown in China's aluminum production had the industry on edge long before the release of this report. This year, aluminum production in China will likely grow by barely 2%. Next year, some experts predict the figures could be even worse. Meanwhile, the situation remains extremely fluid, especially after the U.S. recently announced the imposition of a 25% tariff on steel and aluminum imports from all other countries, including China.

Salient Features of the New Plan

The new Chinese roadmap stresses better and more eco-friendly output where aluminum is concerned, as well as an improvement of the resilience and security of the aluminum supply chain. According to reports, the blueprint also spelled out targets along the two-year timeline for boosting the robustness of the raw material supply chain, using technology to innovate the production and promoting sustainable production.

The plan also includes proposals to expand processing hubs. This includes boosting energy-efficient electrolytic aluminum capacity to over 30%, increasing clean energy use to at least 30% and enhancing red mud recycling to over 15%.

Technological advancements will likewise focus on breakthroughs in low-carbon smelting and precision processing, driving new growth in aluminum consumption. The goal is to establish a high-quality development framework by 2035, ensuring China remains a global aluminum market leader.

The Bauxite Race

The reality of today's aluminum market is that everybody, including China and the U.S., is looking around for high-quality bauxite ore. For example, China's bauxite imports in the first nine months of 2024 were 20% higher than the previous year. This report, quoting S&P Global, said Beijing's dependence on imported bauxite was about 57%, with most of its supply coming in from Guinea.

However, disruptions in that supply chain, coupled with cutbacks by other suppliers like Australia, has left China and the rest of the world looking for other global suppliers.

An article in Forbes said that in the last two years, the price of Guinea ore increased to US $75/t, a rise of about 50%. Meanwhile, Australian ore price increased 60% at $55/t. Alumina and aluminum prices have also climbed, with the former surging 140% in the past year due to supply disruptions and refinery shutdowns in Australia.

Likely Impact of the New Plan

The slowdown in aluminum production, the deletion of tax rebates on exports and a bevy of other factors are bound to cut down China's export numbers in the coming weeks. This, in turn, will negatively impact aluminum prices. With the announcement of the "aluminum sector makeover" plan, production dynamics will shift as China stresses cleaner production and higher-quality ores.

Under the new plan, China clearly wants to cut reliance on bauxite by boosting recycling, aiming for a 15 MT increase in recycled aluminum output. Much of the plan focuses on improving energy efficiency by investing in tech to cut energy use and lower emissions for this sector.

The opinion is that it's not merely about producing cleaner aluminum, but that China is jockeying to make aluminum more sustainable so it can maintain its global position at the top of the aluminum market.

How Will the Changes Affect the U.S.?

Like China, the U.S. also relies heavily on bauxite imports. Last year, import figures for crude and dried bauxite ore into the U.S. saw a slight drop, though it was higher in the fourth quarter. For its part, the United States mainly imports from countries like Jamaica and Turkey.

With China's new plan, it is important to keep an eye on U.S. aluminum imports from China, Canada and several other countries. Given the ever-changing tariff scenario, the slowing down of China's aluminum sector, the new China plan and other factors, the U.S. may have to start looking for other sources to fill the expected import shortfall.
